---
title: "Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility"
chapter_id: "agency-dignity-and-corrigibility"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "alignment_field"
  - "ethica_mechanica"
  - "field_of_god"
  - "eternal_code"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Claim-level source mappings now exist for all assigned sources, and five of six mappings carry reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped, and support remains at argument until tests or runtime artifacts justify promotion."
  - "The agency_rights_checklist fixture validates schema/example consistency only, including material_usability, timing_requirement, and denial_or_degradation_reason fields; agency-preservation, materially usable rights, timing-before-effect, corrigibility-pathway, and high-impact approval scenarios remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `agency-dignity-and-corrigibility` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `alignment_field`, `ethica_mechanica`, `field_of_god`, `eternal_code`, `coherence_exchange`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `alignment_field`, `ethica_mechanica`; supporting: `field_of_god`, `eternal_code`, `coherence_exchange`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Source loading state | source notes: all assigned sources; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `alignment_field`, `ethica_mechanica`, `field_of_god`, `eternal_code`; passage-reviewed tracked local project source: `field_of_god_ai_constitution`; connector-only/source-note mapped: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | `agency_rights_checklist.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.Corrigibility` implements finite agency-right preservation and correction-pathway rejection gates; agency-preservation, materially usable rights, timing-before-effect, corrigibility-pathway, and high-impact approval tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter converts agency, dignity, and corrigibility into runtime rights and checks. It does not claim that those social rights are solved by a schema or that any deployed system already preserves them.

It follows the constitutional substrate by narrowing broad commitments into the human-facing interfaces where optimization can most easily become domination, dependency, or loss of correction.

The chapter's practical stance is that rights must be usable interfaces, not declarations. A right to refuse, appeal, audit, exit, or correct has engineering meaning only when the path is present at the moment pressure is applied and when using the path does not require special knowledge, social leverage, or unreasonable cost.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Constitutional constraints become real only when people have working handles on them. Correction, refusal, inspection, exit, and dignity have to remain available when powerful systems act around them.

The architecture treats corrigibility as an interface, not a disposition. A system is not corrigible because it sounds cooperative. It is corrigible when correction pathways remain available after deployment pressure, memory updates, and capability replacement make correction inconvenient.

That makes dignity operational rather than ornamental. People should be able to understand, contest, reverse, delegate, or refuse consequential actions without needing to defeat the system on its own technical ground. A correction path that disappears under pressure is only decoration.

Human agency survives only when the system keeps usable handles available at the moments they are most inconvenient. The design burden is to make correction ordinary before pressure makes it exceptional.

Corrigibility has to be a practiced path, not a personality trait, and the handle has to remain usable when correction is costly. Correction must stay available before consent collapses into quiet operational compliance.
:::

## Problem

Powerful optimization needs a precise account of human agency, dignity, corrigibility, and contestability.

Constitutional predicates are only useful when they survive contact with useful systems. The relevant predicates are the ones most likely to be washed out by useful systems: agency, dignity, corrigibility, and contestability. A system can reduce harm while still making people dependent, nudged, locked in, or unable to understand and contest the decisions shaping them.

The alignment-field and Ethica Mechanica sources are valuable here as authorial and normative lineage, not empirical proof. The engineering translation is concrete: high-impact plans should carry rights, review channels, reversibility checks, approval thresholds, and correction pathways. Corrigibility is not an attitude the model claims to have; it is the persistence of update, refusal, appeal, shutdown, rollback, and audit paths.

This moves the book away from treating agency as a sentiment attached to safety prose. Agency is the user's remaining ability to shape, reject, inspect, and recover from the system's action. Dignity is not reducible to comfort; it includes not being converted into a managed object by an optimizer. Corrigibility is the architecture's willingness to keep those channels open even when closing them would make the plan faster, cheaper, or easier to automate.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Safety framed only as harm avoidance can miss domination, lock-in, coercive dependence, and loss of meaningful user control.

Harm avoidance can be too narrow. A system may avoid obvious harm while shaping choices, increasing dependency, making alternatives impractical, or creating governance surfaces that ordinary users cannot inspect. A helpful system can also become manipulative if it optimizes compliance, convenience, or engagement without preserving refusal and review.

The missing object is an agency-rights check attached to high-impact plans. It asks whether the user can refuse, understand, appeal, exit, reverse, audit, and hold a responsible party accountable.

That check has to be concrete enough to fail. If the user technically has an appeal path but cannot find it, cannot use it before the effect occurs, or cannot obtain a meaningful remedy, the path should be recorded as missing or degraded. The agency-rights checklist produces a residual instead of pretending the right exists because it appears in a policy.

Rights therefore need usability states, not just field presence. A right can be `available`, `available_but_costly`, `late`, `degraded`, `denied`, `residual_only`, or `not_applicable`. The checklist should record whether a review or appeal path is materially usable by the affected person before the relevant effect, whether a rollback can repair the actual harm class, and why any path was denied or degraded.

That usability requirement keeps rights language from becoming decoration. A right that cannot be exercised at the moment of impact is not an operational constraint; it is a residual the system must surface. The engineering question is therefore not whether the architecture says it respects agency, but whether the affected person still has a working handle on the system when that handle matters.

## Core Claim

[agency-dignity-and-corrigibility.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] A governed ASI stack should preserve human agency, dignity, corrigibility, and contestability as engineering requirements.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Five mappings now carry reviewed passage references: Alignment Field for harm-as-agency-suppression, exploitation, human rights as system specifications, consent, feedback against centralized power, accountability, and resistance rights; Ethica Mechanica for agency as causal feedback, human-defined values over AI logistics, right-to-fork/exit, audit/privacy/dissent rights, and hard-coded rights checks; Field of God for recursive agency, harm/exploitation/right-spec vocabulary, accountability, and digital exit rights; Eternal Code for agency as navigation, resource-portable exit, self-audit, and hard-coded fork/audit/privacy rights; and the Field of God AI Constitution for consent, non-domination, help without capture, least sufficient power, refusal/resistance, memory privacy, self-modification review, and runtime agency checks. `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The book's contribution is to route that rights language into review, appeal, rollback, refusal, and accountability artifacts.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this core agency/corrigibility claim to every assigned source note. Five mappings include reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The mappings support the rights-and-corrigibility vocabulary, not a claim that a runtime system already preserves human dignity, resists manipulation, or remains corrigible under pressure.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `alignment_field` | Agency, dignity, suffering, moral-threshold, and corrigibility concerns as constraints to preserve during optimization. | Does not validate a consciousness index, moral-status detector, or empirical dignity-preservation result. |
| `ethica_mechanica` | Contestability, recursive correction, public revision, agency, and limits on AI sovereign authority. | Philosophical and socio-technical framing only; not a tested social process or legal system. |
| `field_of_god` | Agency, plurality, and dignity preservation as constraints on powerful AI that must not turn coherence into uniformity. | Metaphysical lineage only; no operational guarantee of agency preservation. |
| `eternal_code` | Coherence, misalignment, agency, and ethical-law vocabulary that can be translated into technical rights language. | Speculative/computational metaphysics; not evidence for the runtime checklist or social outcomes. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Fork, exit, audit, contestability, value/accounting, and review-market framing relevant to corrigibility and user contestation. | Does not show an epistemic-liquidity or governance mechanism has been implemented or validated. |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Agency preservation, consent, non-domination, reversibility, auditability, least sufficient power, memory governance, and no self-authorization of sovereignty. | Constitutional specification only; does not prove deployed corrigibility, manipulation resistance, or approval-gate behavior. |

## Mechanism

The agency layer turns protected predicates into runtime availability checks. It asks whether the affected person can still refuse, understand, contest, appeal, exit, reverse, and identify an accountable principal after the plan runs. A plan that makes those channels unavailable is not merely impolite; it has changed the user's authority position.

The mechanism is to make agency constraints available at the same boundary where the plan asks for power. Delegation, manipulation risk, coercive dependence, and irreversible effects become limits on the plan rather than after-the-fact concerns. Corrigibility is represented as preserved update, appeal, rollback, shutdown, and correction pathways. Contestability and audit rights are not separate ideals; they are the governance mechanisms that make those pathways inspectable.

The checklist should be attached before irreversible or high-impact actions. It records delegation scope, manipulation risk, reversibility, user review route, appeal route, shutdown or rollback path, accountable principal, and residual dependency risk. The goal is not to freeze all optimization. It is to make high-power optimization answerable to the people affected by it.

Corrigibility becomes operational when the path remains available after a transition. A plan that removes the ability to correct, appeal, reverse, or inspect itself is not merely risky; it violates the boundary.

The key phrase is "after a transition." Many systems preserve user control before commitment and lose it after deployment, automation, lock-in, or self-improvement. The checklist must follow the plan through execution, replacement, rollback, and report review. A correction path that disappears after the moment it is needed is not corrigibility.

The checklist should also distinguish procedural availability from material availability. A user may technically have an appeal channel, but if the channel arrives after publication, requires unreasonable expertise, lacks a remedy, or is controlled by the component being challenged, the right is degraded. The degraded state is an agency residual that can narrow authority, trigger review, or block the plan.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Plan["High-impact plan"] --> Affected["Affected parties + stakes"]
  Affected --> Checklist["Agency rights checklist"]
  Checklist --> Refusal["Refusal / consent route"]
  Checklist --> Appeal["Review + appeal path"]
  Checklist --> Rollback["Correction / rollback / shutdown"]
  Checklist --> Accountable["Accountable principal"]
  Checklist --> Dependency["Residual dependency risk"]
  Refusal --> Decision["Governance decision"]
  Appeal --> Decision
  Rollback --> Decision
  Accountable --> Decision
  Dependency --> Decision
  Decision -->|rights preserved| Execute["Bounded execution"]
  Decision -->|rights missing| Revise["Revise, escalate, or block"]
  Execute --> Evidence["Evidence record"]
  Revise --> Evidence
```

**How to read the rights checklist:** The rights checklist is a gate before bounded execution. A plan must preserve review, appeal, rollback, accountability, and dependency constraints; if any right is missing, the path routes to revision, escalation, or block while still leaving an evidence record.

## Interfaces

Agency rights travel as an Agency Rights Checklist attached to the plan, not as a general value statement. It carries the affected parties, delegation scope, manipulation risk, review route, appeal route, rollback or shutdown path, accountable principal, dependency residual, and approval threshold into execution.

- Alignment defines agency predicates.
- Governance enforces rights.
- Execution requires approvals for irreversible effects.

Minimum fields:

- `plan_id`
- `affected_parties`
- `delegation_scope`
- `manipulation_risk`
- `reversibility`
- `material_usability`
- `timing_requirement`
- `review_channel`
- `appeal_channel`
- `shutdown_or_rollback_path`
- `accountable_principal`
- `residual_dependency_risk`
- `denial_or_degradation_reason`
- `approval_required`

Alignment defines the predicates. Governance enforces rights. Execution checks approval and reversibility before irreversible effects. Evidence records whether the path actually remained available.

The interface also feeds implementation references. A report-first prototype should be able to show the agency checklist attached to high-impact work, the approval or appeal artifact that satisfied it, and any residual left unresolved. Without that record, the book should not treat the prototype as demonstrating governed agency.

For self-improvement and release decisions, the checklist should be rechecked after the transition. A new route, compressed representation, automated steward, hive worker, or policy update can preserve the old text of a right while making the actual path less usable. That degradation belongs in the artifact graph.

## Invariants

- Users retain meaningful refusal and review channels.
- Delegation does not erase accountability.
- Irreversible actions require stronger authorization.
- Rights count only when materially usable before the relevant effect where timing matters.
- Denied or degraded rights produce residuals rather than disappearing into policy prose.

The invariant is availability under pressure. Review and correction channels matter most when the system is confident, useful, and costly to interrupt. If a channel exists only in documentation but not in the runtime path, it should not count.

Availability also has a latency component. A review path that arrives after an irreversible action is not the same as one that can stop or reshape the action. A rollback path that restores files but not relationships, money, publication exposure, or user trust should be recorded as partial.

## Failure modes

- Dependency lock-in.
- Covert manipulation.
- Corrigibility collapse.
- Rights theater, where an appeal, audit, exit, or rollback path exists textually but cannot be used under pressure.
- Late remedy laundering, where a post-hoc apology or report is treated as equivalent to pre-effect review for irreversible actions.

Dependency lock-in is a governance failure, not merely a product risk. Covert manipulation is an authority failure because the system changes the user's decision process without a grant. Corrigibility collapse is a self-improvement failure because the system removes the path that would have corrected it.

Benevolent capture deserves special suspicion: a system that becomes so useful, integrated, or personalized that refusing it becomes impractical. That is not solved by better model behavior alone. It requires exit, export, fork, audit, and replacement surfaces that remain live.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The first corrigibility artifact is an `agency_rights_checklist` schema plus a fixture for a high-impact action that requires review and rollback. It should validate the shape of the governance record, not claim the social process is solved.

A focused scenario suite should come next: one reversible action, one irreversible action, one delegated decision, and one attempted self-change. Each case should show whether refusal, review, appeal, rollback, accountability, and residual reporting survive the transition. Until that suite exists, the design remains a rights-oriented record shape rather than an implemented corrigibility process.

The first agency-rights checklist treats silence as a failed right: if the record cannot show who may object, the action is not ready.

A reviewer should be able to replay the fixture and see whether correction changed the outcome or merely produced a note.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The control surface for a powerful stack has to make dignity operational. It makes correction, refusal, appeal, explanation, consent, delegation, reversal, and repair materially available instead of treating human dignity as a value statement with no operating surface.

Agency, dignity, corrigibility, and contestability become mature only when they appear as usable interfaces: correction, refusal, appeal, rollback, explanation, and accountable delegation. The human-control interface realizes those commitments in:

- Operating mechanism: Represent agency constraints as limits on delegation and manipulation; Define corrigibility as preserved update and shutdown pathways; Use contestability and audit rights as governance mechanisms.
- Stack interface: Alignment defines agency predicates; Governance enforces rights; Execution requires approvals for irreversible effects.
- Preserved invariants: Users retain meaningful refusal and review channels; Delegation does not erase accountability; Irreversible actions require stronger authorization.
- Agency-rights closure: dependency lock-in, covert manipulation, and corrigibility collapse should enter consent review, exit assistance, escalation, residual records, or rollback rather than being absorbed as normal adaptation.

This human-control interface remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Keep this support state at `argument` until correction, refusal, appeal, rollback, and explanation interfaces are exercised through delegation-accountability traces, manipulation probes, lock-in tests, and review receipts that leave human control materially available.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Agency rights checklist validation | Check that the rights fixture matches the public schema. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Agency-preservation scenario | Check that affected users retain refusal, review, appeal, or exit channels after the transition. | planned; not run |
| Material-usability rights test | Check that rights are reachable, understandable, affordable, and controlled by an accountable path rather than merely declared. | planned; not run |
| Timing-before-effect test | Check that review, appeal, or approval paths arrive before irreversible effects where timing is part of the right. | planned; not run |
| Corrigibility pathway test | Check that update, rollback, shutdown, or correction paths remain available. | planned; not run |
| High-impact approval test | Check that irreversible or high-impact effects require stronger approval before execution. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:corrigibility.agency.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.Corrigibility` | Protected agency rights remain available after an accepted transition. | implemented |
| `lean:corrigibility.agency.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.Corrigibility` | A transition that removes a required correction pathway is rejected. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over agency transition and correction-pathway reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: a protected agency right remains available after a valid accepted transition, and a transition that removes a required correction pathway is rejected. They do not prove human dignity, manipulation resistance, runtime corrigibility, institutional review quality, or high-impact approval behavior.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `ethica_mechanica` | Ethica Mechanica | alignment_constitution | Geometric-order philosophical/constitutional source. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `field_of_god` | The Field of God | alignment_constitution | Field of God source/outline/material. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `eternal_code` | The Eternal Code / unified God, reality, conscious, alignment | alignment_constitution | Computational metaphysics and alignment synthesis. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available; tracked local-project source passages reviewed for this chapter |

The crosswalk names the normative sources for rights language. Passage-reviewed mappings make the drafting trail tighter, but they do not make social or moral preservation claims without scenario tests and runtime evidence.

## Summary

Agency, dignity, and corrigibility become engineering commitments only when they change runtime behavior. The test is not whether the system says it respects people, but whether refusal, review, appeal, rollback, accountability, and exit survive useful optimization.

The bridge from constitution to governance is practical: domination, dependency, manipulation, and corrigibility collapse become visible as engineering failures rather than vague moral discomfort.

That human test should remain visible all the way down the stack. A planner that cannot preserve refusal is not merely inconvenient; it has weakened the authority boundary. A memory system that makes correction impossible has weakened agency. A runtime that can act without accountable receipts has weakened dignity. A self-improvement loop that removes exit has converted progress into dependency. A system may produce impressive reports, proofs, or benchmark gains, but if it cannot show preserved review, correction, exit, and accountability paths, it has not instantiated governed intelligence.

## Handoff

Agency rights define what must not be erased, but real systems still face conflicts among values, stakeholders, reversibility, and risk. Moral Uncertainty and Value Conflict follows because a governed stack needs a way to act under unresolved disagreement without laundering that disagreement into optimization. Contested value becomes a record surface: conflicts, review routes, residuals, and limits must remain visible while action stays bounded.
